mnemonics.title: Authentication Factors
mnemonics.content: KHB - Know (Password), Have (Token), Be (Biometric) - Remember: 'Kids Have Books' for KHB

mnemonics.title: OAuth Flow Steps
mnemonics.content: PAR - Permission, Authorization, Resource access. Think: 'Please Allow Request' for OAuth authorization flow

mnemonics.title: Zero Trust Principles
mnemonics.content: NVV - Never Trust, Always Verify, Verify Explicitly. Remember: 'No Very Vague' for strict verification

mnemonics.title: IAM Components
mnemonics.content: IUP - Identity Provider, User, Policy. Think: 'Identify Users Properly' for IAM components

acronyms.usage: Security mechanism requiring multiple verification methods
Framework for managing digital identities and access rights
Identity layer built on OAuth 2.0 for authentication
Compact token format for secure information transmission
Dynamic authentication based on risk assessment

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hotel key card system: User (guest) asks Front Desk (Authorization Server) for access → gets Key Card (token) → shows to Room (Resource Server) → gets service. The room never sees the guest's ID directly.
Think of MFA like a bank vault: 1st lock needs key (password), 2nd needs code (token), 3rd needs fingerprint (biometric). All three must unlock access.
Picture a museum with guards at every door, not just at the entrance. Every room entry requires checking credentials - no one gets automatic trust just because they passed the lobby.
Imagine a universal passport: One ID works across multiple countries (organizations). The home country vouches for you to other countries without them maintaining their own verification.

analogies.analogy: Like a movie ticket - you buy it once (login), show it at entry (request with token), get into the movie (access resource). The ticket is validated each time without re-entering payment info.
Like a restaurant tab - you sign in once (open tab), everything you order (API requests) is tracked under your tab, and when you leave (logout), the tab is closed.
OAuth is like giving a valet your car key (limited access to move car only), while SAML is like giving someone your full ID (complete identity assertion).

Like recognizing a friend's face - your brain naturally identifies unique features without needing them to state their name. Biometrics work similarly - unique physical traits identify you automatically.
